observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the City Treasurer (CT) to (a) ensure that cash-handling AOs are sufficiently bonded and that cash advances granted to DOs are limited to their maximum accountability; (b) deposit all collections intact with the City’s authorized government depository bank (AGDB) daily, or at the least, the next banking day, in accordance with Section 32 of MNGAS for LGUs; and (c) require all field collectors to prepare and submit their individual RAAF at the end of each month to facilitate preparation and submission of CRAAF by the CT to the AT.
observations.reason: According to the CTO, the failure to renew the fidelity bonds of AOs concerned was due to an oversight. CTO officer explained that delays in depositing some collections were due to some COs not completing their RCDs in time for the required deposit schedule. Specifically, COs need to balance their collections against the ORs, which is a time-consuming process. Additionally, their concurrent responsibility of attending to taxpayers further contributes to delays in the preparation of RCDs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE require the (a) CGSO Officer to (i) conduct a physical count of inventories and report the results to the CAO using the RPCI, on a semestral basis for reconciliation of property records with the CAO; (ii) limit the procurement of inventories equivalent to two-month to prevent excess issuance to end-users; and (iii) prepare the SSMI together with the RIS and submit them to the CAO for recording of issued inventories in the books; (b) CA and CGSO Officer to maintain SLCs and SCs, respectively, to support the general ledger account balances; (c) CDRRMO Chief to strengthen internal controls on re-ordering welfare goods for stock to prevent prolonged storage in stockroom leading to near-expiry welfare goods; and (d) CA to follow the PIM in the recording and issuance of inventories.
observations.reason: Due to the absence of an accurate report on inventory on hand, whether generated by the PIAS, or prepared manually, that can be used for conducting physical inventory counts at any given period, the CGSO was not able to carry out periodic inventory counts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE require the (a) IC to (i) accelerate joint efforts to reconcile discrepancies between accounting records and results of physical inventory; (ii) maintain complete PCs and PPELCs for monitoring of PPEs; (iii) conduct physical count of PPE at least once a year to establish the accuracy and existence of PPE account balances; and (iv) establish the final appraisal value of the properties for disposal to determine the minimum selling price, ensuring fair compensation for the government; (b) CA to review and analyze the cost of completed projects still recorded in the CIP account and reclassify them to their appropriate PPE account supported with Certificate of Completion; and (c) CGSO Officer to (i) renew the registration of all serviceable motor vehicles of the City with the LTO to avoid unnecessary expenses from payment of damages and penalties in case of vehicular accidents; and (ii) maintain a permanent file of the OR-CR of all motor vehicles owned by the City, and henceforth maintain an archival system to ensure safety and protection of all public documents and records.
observations.reason: While efforts were made to cleanse the PPE accounts in the CAO’s GL during the year, the process was not completed in the PIAS used by the CGSO. The cleansing process, which includes reconciling, verifying, and removing semi-expandable items and erroneous entries, was hindered by resource constraints and a lack of dedicated personnel.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE require the (a) CDRRMO Officer to prepare and submit the LDRRMFIP in accordance with the format provided in Annex A of COA Circular No. 2012-002 to reflect the QRF and PMF, as well as the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended CDRRMF of previous years; and (b) CA and CT to perform reconciliation of the recorded Trust Liabilities – DRRMF account with the CIB-DRRMF account and make the necessary adjustments on the identified discrepancy to reflect the correct amount of unexpended CDRRMF in the financial statements at year-end.
observations.reason: The CAO personnel have yet to reconcile the noted variances due to a lack of dedicated personnel to conduct the reconciliation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE (a) strictly enforce the settlement of the audit disallowances that were not appealed, as the prescribed periods for settlement have already lapsed; and (b) require the (i) City Officials involved in the processing of claims to be more diligent in evaluating the completeness of supporting documents; and (ii) persons liable to immediately refund the disallowances once they have become final and executory.
observations.managementAction: The LCE will require the City Officials involved in the processing of claims to exercise greater diligence in evaluating the completeness and validity of supporting documents before approving any claims; and The LCE will ensure that legal action is taken against responsible parties, and the status of these legal cases will be reported regularly.
observations.reason: With Motion for Reconsideration filed by persons liable.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that Management prioritize conducting a comprehensive physical inventory to address the accumulated backlog of assets and the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances under the guidelines and procedures stated in COA Circular No. 2020-006, dated January 31, 2020.
Dedicate sufficient resources and personnel to complete the inventory process efficiently.
observations.managementAction: Likewise, the Municipal Mayor enforce strict requirements for the inventory committee to carry out the inventory taking. Provide clear directives, allocate necessary resources, and monitor progress to ensure timely completion of inventory tasks.
observations.reason: Reiterated in CY 2024 Annual Audit Report (AAR).

observations.recommendation: We reiterate our recommendation that the Acting Municipal Treasurer updates her cashbook regularly and furnish the Municipal Accountant with the cashbook balances of the Cash in Local Treasury under the three funds at least quarterly. Failing this, we recommend that the Local Chief Executive appoints a dependable Liquidating Officer to oversee the collection and deposit processes, emphasizing the significance of accurate and updated cashbook records.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant and the Acting Municipal Treasurer immediately reconcile the Cash-Local Treasury account's subsidiary ledger and cashbook quarterly in compliance with Item 6.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
observations.reason: The Municipal Treasurer is not promptly submitting the cashbook preventing the timely reconciliation.

observations.recommendation: a. Deposit all her collections intact the following day in consonance with the existing rules and regulations; b. Record collections turned over by the Collecting Clerks and update entries in the cashbook daily and reconcile the cashbook balance of cash on hand with the subsidiary ledger of the Accounting Office quarterly; and c. Stop the practice of accommodating chits or promissory notes of Municipal Officials out of her collections.
observations.reason: The same practices were still noted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Acting Municipal Treasurer agreed to strictly enforce the regulation on the daily turnover of collections intact and refrain from issuing additional Accountable Forms if unremitted collections remain in the collector’s possession.
observations.managementAction: In cases of persistent or intentional delays in the remittances of collections, the Acting Municipal Treasurer immediately refers the matter to the Municipal Mayor and recommends the relief of defiant collecting officer.
observations.reason: The same practices were still noted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Development Council evaluate the viability of the previous year’s projects otherwise, direct the Municipal Engineer to expedite the implementation of the previous year’s 20% DF projects. Likewise, the Municipal Mayor instructs the Municipal Engineer and other offices overseeing the 20% DF projects to update the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator regarding fund utilization. This will enable the coordinator to prepare and submit the necessary status and accomplishment reports. Furthermore, the Municipal Mayor foster collaboration among offices, implement regular monitoring, and streamline the processes to accelerate the implementation of the 20% DF that was unexpended in prior years.
observations.reason: Reiterated in CY 2024 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: Augment the staffing within the Accounting Unit or the General Services Office to include dedicated personnel responsible for maintaining the repair history ledger of heavy and transportation units. Ensure that these personnel actively utilize the ledger to track maintenance history. Likewise, incorporate the corresponding cost of repairs into the history ledger to quantify the specific expenses incurred for each piece of equipment. Establish a protocol to promptly notify the Motor Pool of any identified recurring repair and maintenance issues. This will enable the Motor Pool to conduct thorough investigations into the underlying causes of frequent repairs, including potential issues with specific vehicle models, components, or operational practices. By addressing these underlying problems effectively, the Motor Pool can implement proactive measures to reduce repair frequency and enhance the overall reliability of the equipment fleet.
observations.managementAction: A new GSO has recently been appointed.
observations.reason: The Accounting Unit has not received additional staff to address these audit issues.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management, through the Municipal Vice Mayor, require the VMLP, PCL, and LESSON to provide an accounting of the financial assistance granted by the proponent. The proponent of the financial assistance requires the private foundation to submit a report detailing how this fund was utilized. Henceforth, Management ensures that financial assistance granted to government and private organizations is covered with MOA, and the Municipal Accountant records this financial assistance as inter-agency receivables to enhance accountability over the funds utilized.
observations.reason: The same practice was still noted.

observations.recommendation: Direct the focal person or Implementing Offices to furnish the Accounting Office copies of MOA or other pertinent documents related to these fund transfers, submit to the Fund Accounting Office the Fund Utilization Report after the completion of the program or project so appropriate adjustment could be taken up in the books; and Direct the Municipal Accountant to review the outstanding balances of the Due to NGAs account, determine whether the projects are already completed or not, secure MOA and other relevant documents to support the subsidiary records and record the FUR submitted by the Implementing Offices and for FT without supporting details and had been long outstanding in the books, recommend or initiate the write-off of these accounts following the procedures under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016, if warranted.
observations.reason: Not all financial assistance received by the LGU were accompanied by a MOA. The submission of the utilization reports was not yet submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the Land account per accounting records with the list of land from the Municipal Assessor’s Office and provide a detailed schedule on the balance of land account after the Inventory Committee conducted the physical count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) in order to have a fair presentation of said account in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Inclusion of new land records is now backed by the appropriate supporting documents.
observations.reason: Balances pertaining to previous years remained unsubstantiated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: a.(cid:9)Require the City Accountant to recognize in the books of account the receipt of drugs and medicines from various sources for CY 2024 totaling ₱17.794 million; b.(cid:9)Require the City Health Officer to provide the City Accountant an updated report on ending inventory as of December 31, 2024 for all received drugs and medicines from various sources including their total cost; c.(cid:9)Require the City Accountant to adjust the balance of drugs and medicines inventory account upon receipt of the updated report of ending inventory from the City Health Office; and d.(cid:9)Likewise, require the City Health Officer to furnish immediately the City Accountant all necessary documents regarding the receipt of grants and donations in kind in order for the City Accountant to recognize in the books of accounts on a timely basis in compliance with Paragraphs 96 and 97 of the International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) No. 23.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant: a.(cid:9)To prepare the necessary adjusting entries for the proper recognition of Other payables accounts and identify all reconciling items under the Other Payable account and effect the necessary adjustments, if any, to properly present the balance in the financial statements; and b.(cid:9)To identify all reconciling items under Other Payable account and effect the necessary adjustments, if any, to properly present the balance in the financial statements.
We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries for the proper classification of various PPE accounts.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to record the uninstalled house numbering plates as Other Supplies and Materials Inventory in the amount ₱2,222,350.00 to arrive at an accurate balance of the account; 1) Create an Inventory Team 2) Conduct inventory of uninstalled numbering plates 3) Prepare adjusting entries for the uninstalled plates.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries for the proper recognition of the Road Network account and the corresponding depreciation expense.
We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries for the proper classification of the accounts.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to adjust the recorded balance of the Breeding stocks account and the Work/Zoo Animals account to arrive at an accurate balance of the affected accounts.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to 1)(cid:9)To evaluate dormant receivable accounts in which collectability are already remote after exhausting all possible means and file a request for qualified authority to write-off to the Commission on Audit (COA) pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 and COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023; 2)(cid:9)To exhaust all possible means; 3)(cid:9)To file a request for authority to write-off to the COA.
We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entry for the proper classification of the accounts.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management require the City General Services Officer in coordination with the City Legal Officer and City Assessor to prioritize the registration and transfer of the titles by securing all necessary documentary requirements to facilitate the titling process to ensure ownership and to fully protect the interest of the City in compliance with Section 39 of P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended that Management coordinate with various agencies like Philippine Statistics Authority (PSA) and Department of Social Welfare and Development (DSWD) to leverage reliable data on the actual number of families and houses, which will help in better planning, allocation of resources, and the smooth rollout of the house numbering system project. Moreover, we recommended that Management ensure the implementation and completion of all the LGU’s programs, projects and activities to immediately serve the intended purpose or use thereof to the constituents.
We recommended that Management ensure that all designated Special Disbursing Officers secure fidelity bonds and submit a copy to the Auditor’s Office for verification.

We recommended that Management require the project coordinator to submit a detailed explanation for the inefficiency observed in the installation of the house numbering plates and consider the possibility of engaging a third-party contractor to expedite the installation process. Moreover, we recommended that management refund the excessive expenditures amounting to ₱94,250.00.

We recommended that Management ensure that all disbursements/utilizations of Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund is in conformity with the guidelines set by National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council, Department of Budget and Management and Department of the Interior and Local Government (NDRRMC-DBM-DILG) Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.

We recommended that Management utilize all funds received to serve its intended purpose in order to immediately deliver the benefits to the intended recipients/beneficiaries. Also, determine the validity of the payables so that the fund should be paid to the claimant, returned to the source agency or reverted to unappropriated surplus for proper appropriation.

We recommended that Management instruct: a.(cid:9)The City Accountant to reconcile the balances with the concerned agencies (GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth), remit the balances and effect the necessary adjustments in the books of account, as necessary; and b.(cid:9)The City Human Resource Management Officer to regularly update the concerned agencies for any changes in employee status such as separations, retirements, recruitments, and salary adjustments to ensure correct remittance to be made.
We recommended that Management open a separate bank account for the collections of LGU’s share from Lotto and STL operation upon authorization by the Sangguniang Panglungsod.

We recommended that Management coordinate with the respective suppliers/contractors for the release of guaranty deposit payables upon final acceptance of the works or lapse of warranty period to prevent the accumulation of the balances of the liability account.

We recommended that Management formally request to the Sanguniang Panlungsod/Panlalawigan to promptly promulgate an ordinance updating the Schedule of Market Values (SMV) and undertake the general revision of real property assessments in compliance with Section 219 of Republic Act No. 7160 to generate additional funds to finance developmental projects and augment the special education fund.

1.3. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the Officer-
in-Charge (OIC) of the CTO to (a) instruct the AACT to (i) verify the individual ORs
against the RCDs of COs; (ii) consistently reconcile the collections with the deposits
to prevent discrepancies and enhance the accuracy of the cashbooks; (iii) update the
cashbooks for CLT and CIB accounts regularly; (iv) ensure that collections are
deposited intact daily or not later than the next banking day; and (v) implement
procedures to streamline the preparation of RCDs and balancing of collections
against ORs to prevent delays in depositing collections; (b) closely monitor the fidelity
bonds of all AOs to ensure their timely renewal; (c) implement improvements to the
security of the cash vault to prevent unauthorized access and theft; and (d) implement
a process to integrate data on the ORs submitted by field COs into the TOIMS for
real-time reporting and monitoring of collections.

2.3. We recommended that the LCE require the (a) City Accountant (CA) to
appropriately record the dishonored checks in the books; and (b) OIC-CTO to
instruct the AACT to (i) forward the returned checks and their corresponding notices
to the CAO immediately upon receipt to facilitate timely recording in the books; (ii)
strengthen the monitoring process of dishonored checks until they are either settled
by the taxpayer or otherwise addressed; (iii) issue a Notice of Dishonor immediately
upon receipt of dishonored checks; and (iv) cancel the ORs covering dishonored
checks pursuant to Section 57 of the MNGAS for LGUs and Sections 85 and 87,
Volume I of the GAAM.

3.4. We recommended that the LCE require the (a) CA to (i) reclassify the amount
of ₱13,173,639.89 related to Investment in Bonds from the CIB account in accordance
with proper accounting standards; and (b) OIC-CTO to (i) obtain a Statement of
Account or other supporting documentation from the PNB to substantiate the amount
of ₱20,961,000.57 received by the CTO in January 2024 to ensure proper recording
in the books; and (ii) instruct the AACT to review and resolve the reconciling items
identified in the BRS that have been outstanding for extended periods by (1) initiating
a formal request with the AGDB, detailing the reconciling items, and requesting
clarification and supporting documents such as returned check advice, and debit and
credit memos; (2) coordinating with the CAO for any remaining discrepancies that
cannot be resolved through bank clarifications, for possible timing differences or past
errors in the posting of transactions; and (3) implementing a system for regularly
reviewing and addressing reconciling items.

4.4. We recommended that the LCE require the OIC-CTO to (a) expedite the
integration of the RPTIs data with the TOIMS to generate an accurate Certified List
of Taxpayers and Certified List of Delinquent Taxpayers to ensure accurate reporting
of RPT/SET receivables; (b) provide the CA with a copy of the Certified List of
Taxpayers on a timely basis for proper recording of the RPT/SET Receivables; (c)
reconcile the year-end balances of RPT/SET Receivable accounts with the Certified
List of Delinquent Taxpayers and investigate any discrepancies for appropriate
action; (d) keep the taxpayers’ index cards updated; and (e) strengthen collection
efforts and implement additional measures to improve collection efficiency and
reduce outstanding RPT/SET Receivable balances.

4.5. Additionally, we recommended that the LCE prioritize the allocation of
additional resources, including staffing and necessary technological tools, to facilitate
the timely updating of the RPTIS, and the smooth integration of RPTIS data with the
TOIMS, to improve the accuracy and reliability of the list of real property taxpayers.

5.4. We recommended that the LCE require the (a) CGS Officer to (i) fully utilize
the PIAS to track inventory movements and generate accurate and reliable inventory
reports by keeping the system updated; (ii) conduct regular physical inventory counts
at least semiannually; (iii) implement a process for reconciling the physical inventory
count with the PIAS records and accounting records; (iv) ensure the preparation and
submission of the RPCI and SSMI within the prescribed deadlines; and (v) instruct
the designated personnel to keep the SCs updated, either in the PIAS or manually;
and (b) CA to (i) conduct an immediate review and adjustment of the Inventory
account to derecognize from the books the distributed inventories totaling
₱13,797,241.00; and (ii) maintain and keep updated the SLCs.

6.5. We recommended that the LCE require the (a) CA and CGS Officer to (i)
promptly complete the reconciliation of variances between their records; and (ii)
regularly update the PPELCs and PCs, respectively; (b) IC to complete the physical
count of PPE within a reasonable timeframe, facilitating the preparation of the
RPCPPE and ensure its submission to the AT within the prescribed period; and (c)
CGS Officer to improve the usage and functionality of the PIAS for accurate and
consistent asset management.

6.6. We also recommended that the LCE allocate adequate resources and
personnel to support the reconciliation, physical count, and on-going maintenance of
PPE records.

7.6. We recommended that the LCE require the (a) CA to (i) review and reclassify
completed projects from the CIP account to the appropriate PPE accounts, supported
by Certificates of Completion, to reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts;
and (ii) coordinate with the City Engineer (CE) to verify project status and ensure
that completed projects are removed from the CIP balance and ongoing projects are
properly recognized; and (b) CE to require Contractors to submit SWAs or progress
billings at year-end as basis for recording the status of projects in the books of
accounts.

8.5. We recommended that the LCE require the (a) OIC-CTO and the CA to align
their classification of business taxes and conduct a reconciliation to ensure consistent
and accurate financial reporting; (b) OIC-CTO to (i) ensure the retention and proper
storage of all necessary documents required for business permit applications and
renewals, in compliance with Section 176 of the City Revenue Code; and (ii) improve
the system of tracking and collecting returned checks by working closely with the
AGDB and BPLO to prevent issuance of permits to businesses with unpaid taxes; and
(c) CTO and BPLO to implement procedures to withhold the issuance of business
permits until tax payments have been fully processed and cleared.

8.6. Additionally, we recommended that the LCE hold the persons responsible for
the non-collection of returned checks totaling ₱197,744.92 in the event that the
business owners who were issued business permits fail to fully settle their tax
obligations.

9.4. We recommended that the LCE require the CSB to (a) provide the CBO, CAO,
and CTO with a copy of the approved SEF Budget within the prescribed period; (b)
ensure the timely submission of the quarterly and annual SEF Utilization Reports to
the DepEd Central Office, Local Sanggunian, and the Regional Offices of the DBM
and DILG; and (c) coordinate with the ITO personnel to ensure that SEF Utilization
Reports are promptly posted and updated on the City’s official website.

10.7. We recommended that the LCE require the (a) City Urban Planning and
Development (CUPD) Officer, City Budget Officer, and CE to enhance their
collaboration to ensure a more effective alignment of the budget with the local
development plans and facilitate better execution of projects; (b) City Housing Board
(CHB) to ensure prompt utilization of the completed housing units for their intended
purpose of relocating informal settlers; (c) implementing units, such as the CEO,
CENRO, and CAgO, to ensure that allocated funds are fully utilized to maximize the
benefits that could be derived from the completed projects; and (d) CE to submit As-
Built Plan to reflect the changes made in the original contract for the project
“Concreting/Construction of Road and Drainage Canal at Sullera St., Barangay
Pandayan”.

11.5. We recommended that the LCE require the (a) CDRRM Officer to (i) consider
allocating a significant portion of the CDRRMF to flood control projects to better
address flood risks; (ii) develop a detailed annual plan for the CDRRMF that allocates
funds more evenly throughout the year for preparedness activities; (iii) prioritize the
implementation of hazard mapping to obtain the necessary data for identifying and
mitigating disaster risks; and (iv) expedite the operationalization of the CDRRMOC
for disaster response and management activities; and (b) CA to (i) adjust the
Buildings and Other Structures account and Accounts Payable at year-end and close
the CIP account for completed projects; (ii) coordinate with the CE for the submission
of status reports on completed and ongoing construction projects at year-end, to
ensure proper recording or closing of the CIP account in accordance with project
progress; and (iii) perform reconciliation of the recorded TL-DRRMF account with
the CIB-DRRMF account and make the necessary adjustments on the identified
discrepancies to reflect the correct amount of unexpended CDRRMF in the financial
statements at year-end.

12.4. We recommended that the LCE require the (a) implementing offices to ensure
that all PPAs are implemented within the specified timeline; and (b) CE to submit the
duly approved Variation Order and As-Built Plan for the project “Construction of 2-
Storey Classroom Building in Barangay Saluysoy”.

13.7. We recommended that the LCE instruct the CHRM Officer to (a) conduct a
review and evaluation of the City’s existing organizational structure to properly
identify and establish the appropriate human resource complement; (b) consider
appointing qualified JO/COS personnel to fill vacant regular positions; (c) ensure
that the hiring of JO/COS personnel is based on necessity of services, qualifications
and performance; and (d) review the ARs submitted by JO/COS personnel and
ensure that outputs reported thereon are quantifiable and with details to substantiate
the services rendered.

14.8. We recommended that the LCE require the CA to (a) further verify the
discrepancies in taxes withheld and remitted for the current and previous years; and
(b) ensure that taxes withheld are remitted to the BIR immediately upon withholding
in compliance with existing regulations.

15.8. We recommended that the LCE require the CA to (a) prioritize the immediate
reconciliation of the prior years’ unreconciled balances and ensure that any
outstanding balances are verified and remitted promptly; and (b) continue the proper
deduction and remittance of GSIS premiums and loan amortizations, including the
government share, within the prescribed period.

16.6. We recommended that the LCE require the CA, in coordination with the
CHRM Officer, to (a) maintain subsidiary records of all members to enable close
monitoring of individual members’ accounts; and (b) ensure timely remittance of all
withheld amounts.

17.5. We recommended that the LCE require the CA, in coordination with the
CHRM Officer, to (a) implement a system to ensure the timely submission of all
required documents, including those for newly recruited personnel and employees
requesting deferments, to avoid delays in remittances; and (b) ensure timely
remittance of all withheld amounts.

18.6. We recommended that the LCE require the (a) GAD Focal Person (i) in
coordination with CUPD Officer to fast track the establishment of a GAD database
for a more gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation; (ii)
closely monitor the implementation of PPAs embodied in the GPB to ensure full
attainment of GAD objectives; and (iii) conduct gender analysis using the HGDG tool
in the attribution of the GPB; and (b) implementing offices to make sure that
expenditures for GAD activities are within the approved budget so as not to
compromise the implementation of other planned activities.

19.4. We recommended that the LCE (a) strictly enforce the settlement of the audit
disallowances that were not appealed, as the prescribed periods for settlement have
already lapsed; (b) file appropriate legal cases against responsible individuals or
entities involved in the fraudulent or improper actions that led to the disallowances,
particularly in cases of fraudulent checks and payment to contractor despite non-
completion of projects, as per the details in ND Nos. 2015-12-024, 2015-12-026, and
2015-12-027; and (c) require the City Officials involved in the processing of claims to
be more diligent in evaluating the completeness of supporting documents.

1.9. We recommended and Management agreed to complete the one-time
cleansing of PPE account balances under COA Circular No. 2020-006
which mandates the write-off of lumped PPEs balances that were
created to transition from the old accounting system to the eNGAS.

2.7. We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to reclassify
Semi-Expendable Properties within the PPE as follows:
• Those that are still in the custody of the GSO, reclassify to the
appropriate Semi-Expendable Property account;
• Those acquired in CY 2024 but were already issued to the end-users,
reclassify to the appropriate Semi-Expendable Property Expense
and reverse the entries for the recognition of depreciation; and
• Those that were acquired prior to CY 2024 and were already issued
to the end-users, charge to Prior Period Adjustment the carrying
amount and close the corresponding accumulated depreciation.

2.8. We further recommended and Management agreed to require the
Inventory Committee to be mindful of Semi-Expendable Properties
during the physical count to properly identify and distinguish them
from PPE.

3.9. We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to the
following:
• adjust the RPT Receivable and Deferred RPT Income in the
General Fund to conform with the records of the Municipal
Treasurer;
• adjust the SET Receivables and the Deferred SET Income in the
Special Education Fund; and
• determine the advance tax payments applicable in future years and
record the Deferred RPT/SET Income the RPT/SET to be earned in
future years.

3.10. We further recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to
furnish the Municipal Accountant a list showing of RPT and SET
collectible at the beginning of every year to facilitate the set-up of RPT
and SET collectible.

4.8. We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to the
following:
• Transfer the costs of completed projects to the appropriate PPE
accounts; and
• Recognize depreciation expense pertaining to prior years as prior
period adjustment.

4.9. We further recommended and the Municipal Engineer agreed to
furnish the Municipal Accountant a list of ongoing infrastructure
projects and the status thereof quarterly. This will help the Municipal
Accountant transfer completed infrastructure projects from the CIP
account to the appropriate PPE account.

5.16. We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to the
following:
• Verify the noted discrepancies noted in the CIB – LCCA account
and prepare the necessary adjusting entries therefor;
• Record the following as adjustments in the CIB – LCCA account:
i. Unrecorded interest income of ₱397,096.23; and
ii. Unrecorded bank charges for the checkbook issuances, totaling
₱4,363.00;
• Ascertain whether replacement checks have been issued for the stale
checks, totaling ₱12.421 million, and make the necessary adjusting
entries accordingly; and
• Prepare Bank Reconciliation Statements within ten (10) days from
receipt of the Bank Statements pursuant to Item 3.2 of COA
Circular No. 96-011.

6.5. We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to the following:
• Prepare a Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD) and submit the same
to the Municipal Accountant on a daily basis;
• Prepare a Report of Checks Issued (RCI) and submit the same to
the Municipal Accountant on a daily basis;
• Deposit all collections with the authorized depository bank daily or
not later than the next banking day to avoid accumulation of
collections; and
• For replacement checks, a certified copy of the Disbursement
Voucher shall be requested from the Municipal Accountant. The
cancelled check shall then be reported and attached to the RCI in
chronological order.

6.6. We further recommended the following to facilitate the prompt
preparation of the Bank Reconciliation Statements, to which the
Municipal Accountant agreed:
• Prepare Journal Entry Vouchers (JEV) for collections and deposits,
on the basis of RCD, and record the same in the Cash Receipt
Journal; and
• Prepare the JEVs for disbursements, on the basis of RCI, and record
the same in the Check Disbursements Journal.

7.9. We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to the
following:
• Reconcile the accounting records with the Municipal Engineer’s
records to identify completed projects that are still recorded as CIP;
• Transfer the balances completed projects to the appropriate PPE
accounts;
• Recognize depreciation expense pertaining to prior years as prior
period adjustment; and
• Conduct a thorough investigation of all unidentified balances within
the CIP accounts to ascertain their nature and apply the proper
accounting treatment thereto.

7.10. We further recommended and the Municipal Engineer agreed to
provide a quarterly status report detailing the progress of
infrastructure projects undertaken by the Municipality.

7.11. We finally recommended and the Municipal Accountant and Municipal
Engineer agreed to devise a system for the monitoring of the completion
of infrastructure projects for the timely reclassification of these projects
from CIP to the appropriate PPE account.

8.8. We recommended and Management agreed to conduct one-time
cleansing of PPE account balances under COA Circular No. 2020-006
to establish more reliable PPE balances. Part of the process in the
completion of the one-time cleansing is the actual inventory taking of
existing PPEs.

9.10. We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
• Establish an agreement with a government bank that will handle the
payroll process;
• Require the JOs and COS workers to open a payroll account to
facilitate the transition from the Cash Advance system to an ATM-
based payroll system;
• Require the HRMO conduct an awareness program for JOs and
COS workers to educate them on the benefits and processes of the
new payroll system; and
• Conduct a training program to the relevant personnel in the
Cashier's Office, Human Resource Management Office, and
Accounting Office regarding the new ATM-based payroll system.

10.14. We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
• Establish a calendar of activities/events and promptly assign
persons responsible therefor;
• Require event organizers to plan the events in advance to give the
BAC more time to complete the procurement process without
delaying the preparation for the events;
• Incorporate in the Municipality’s procurement plans the proposals
of the event organizers for the activities/events;
• Strategically time the procurement activities with the event schedule
to ensure compliance with the procurement process;
• Undertake Early Procurement Activities for the prompt and timely
delivery of Municipality’s projects; and
• Strictly adhere to the Government Procurement Act, which
mandates the use of prescribed procurement methods provided
therein.

11.12. We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
• Require the Municipal Accountant to demand the immediate
liquidation of the cash advance granted, amounting to ₱70,000.00,
for the transport of the donated Patient Transport Vehicle;
• Prohibit the transfer of cash advance from one Accountable Officer
to another;
• Prioritize payment of expenses via check, and the resort to cash
advances be limited only for impractical or impossible scenarios;
and
• Strictly adhere to the liquidation timelines outlined in COA Circular
97-002 for all cash advances granted.

12.13. We recommended and the Municipal Budget Officer agreed to record
obligations upon receipt by the winning bidder of the Notice of Award.
This practice will yield to more reliable budgetary reports which will
help the Municipality make informed financial decisions.

13.11. We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
• The MPDO will ensure the completion of the CDRA and
incorporate the same in developing the CLUP;
• Upon completion of the CDRA and CLUP, the MPDO will furnish
the LDRRMO the results thereof; and
• The LDRRMO will develop and maintain detailed operational
hazard maps and vulnerability assessments. This will ensure that
mitigation projects and preparedness initiatives are precisely
targeted to hazard-prone areas and populations at risk.

14.4. We recommended and Management agreed to require LDRRMO to
formulate a Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan
(LDRRMP) that includes realistic, concrete, time-bound mitigation
projects, within the Municipality's capacity.

14.5. We further recommended and Management agreed to require the Local
Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council the following to ensure
the implementation of the LDRRMP:
• Hold regular meetings specifically dedicated to LDRRMF
utilization, particularly for the 70% pre-disaster activities and long-
term DRRM initiatives;
• Require the LDRRMO to present detailed utilization reports,
including challenges encountered and proposed solutions; and
• Mandate the publication of the LDRRMP, the annual and quarterly
LDRRMF utilization reports, and details of DRRM projects on the
LGU website, bulletin boards, and other public platforms to
encourage citizen oversight and promote transparency.

15.9. We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
• Develop a Realistic and Implementable CDP: Require the MPDC to
prepare a CDP that accurately reflects the Municipality's
aspirations and needs.
• Ensure a Detailed and Specific AIP: Require the MPDC to prepare
a detailed AIP. This plan should outline concrete, specific, and well-
defined priority projects, programs, and activities that directly
contribute to achieving the Municipality's CDP goals.
• Align Projects with the New CDP: Evaluate whether the
unimplemented projects are aligned with the newly formulated CDP
and discontinue those that are not.
• Adhere to Designated Budgetary Functions: Require the MBO to
strictly adhere to their designated functions under RA No. 7160.

16.11. We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
• Provide Sufficient Details of PPAs in the AIP: Require the Municipal
Planning and Development Office to prepare a detailed AIP that
sufficiently describes PPAs to be implemented by the Municipality,
along with their corresponding costs, the intended location, and
other necessary details.
• Secure Separate Sanggunian Authorization for Charges Against
Generic Appropriations: Require the MBO to strictly adhere to
Section 22(c) of RA No. 7160, ensuring that an authorization,
separate from the Appropriation Ordinance, is present before
charging obligations against appropriations described in generic
terms.
• Require Sanggunian Authorization for Payments Charged Against
Generic Appropriations: Mandate the Municipal Accountant to
require the submission of a Sanggunian Authorization as a
supporting document before allowing payments for contracts
charged against generic appropriations.

17.2. We therefore recommended that the Municipality fully utilize the GAD fund
to address gender-related issues effectively.

We recommended that the LCE require the (a) City Accountant (CA) and CT to (i) exhaust all efforts in tracking the RCDs, validated deposit slips, and other relevant documents related to the reported bank reconciling items, as reflected in the BRS since CY 2013; and (ii) have a formal communication with the bank manager to trace the reported bank reconciling items in their System for appropriate action; and (b) CA to make the necessary adjustment in the books for all unreleased checks at the end of the year, supported with the Report of Unreleased Checks certified by the CT.

We recommended that the LCE require the (a) CUPDO Officer to ensure that future transfer of funds which are considered as realignment or augmentation be done by way of appropriation ordinance and submitted to the Sangguniang Panlungsod for review and approval; (b) CUPDO and CBO to strictly monitor the status of Continuing Appropriations; (c) BAC, CE, and CA to submit the supporting documents of transactions with noted documentary deficiencies; and (d) CE to conduct extensive site investigation during the preparation of the detailed engineering and establish the technical viability or success of the project to prevent delay in the completion of PPAs.

We recommended that the LCE require the CA to (a) review the amounts of taxes withheld and remitted/posted in the ledger to trace the noted discrepancy and make the necessary adjustments, if warranted; and (b) strictly comply with BIR No. 2-98 dated April 17, 1998 as amended by BIR RR No. 11-2018 and BIR RMC No. 23-2012 on the correct withholding of taxes and full remittance of the same within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the LCE require the (a) CA to review the amounts of GSIS contributions withheld and remitted/posted in the ledger to trace the discrepancies, if any, and make the necessary adjustments, if warranted; and (b) City Human Resource and Management Office (CHRMO) Officer and CA to strictly comply with RA No. 8291 on the withholding and full remittance of GSIS contributions within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the LCE require the CA to (a) review the contributions withheld and remitted/posted in the ledger to trace discrepancies, if any, and make the necessary adjustments, if warranted; and (b) CHRMO Officer and CA to strictly comply with RA No. 9679 and its IRR on the withholding and full remittance of Pag-IBIG contributions within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the LCE require the CA to (a) review the amounts of PhilHealth contributions withheld and remitted/posted in the ledger to trace the discrepancies, if any, and make the necessary adjustments, if warranted; and (b) CHRMO Officer and CA to strictly comply with RA No. 7875, as amended by RA Nos. 9241 and 10606 and its RIRR on the withholding and full remittance of PhilHealth contributions within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the LCE require the (a) CA to (i) instruct the CAO personnel concerned to maintain SLs for Due to NGAs account; (ii) in coordination with the BAC and CE, submit to the AT the legal, technical, and financial documents of noted transactions to preclude them from being suspended in audit; (iii) ensure that the documentary requirements for the procurement of infrastructure projects with reference to RA No. 9184 are submitted and attached to DVs for timely verification and audit of accounts; and (iv) coordinate with the City Officials concerned to submit the liquidation report with supporting documents of the noted cash advances with documentary deficiencies; and (b) CE to (i) aim for the full utilization and implementation of the identified development projects under the trust fund for optimum attainment of the intent of the fund; and (ii) perform detailed engineering study to identify all potential issued in a project and address them immediately to prevent delays and additional unnecessary cost.

We recommended that the LCE require the (a) CT and CA to reconcile their respective books to resolve the noted discrepancy of ₱37,975,236.77 between the recorded income from business taxes vis-à-vis the report submitted to the BLGF; and (b) CT to (i) keep the subsidiary ledgers of business taxpayers updated to ensure that all business establishments settle their business taxes yearly; (ii) submit a report on delinquent business taxpayers and consider discussing with the software developer any viable way to include the said report in the System; and (iii) enforce available enforcement measures to promote taxpayer compliance with established tax regulations.

We recommended that the LCE require the (a) CENRO to (i) collaborate with punong barangays to strictly enforce the “Yes Segregation, Yes Collection” policy by establishing a team at the barangay level to monitor the waste management practices of households and establishments and impose penalties accordingly; (ii) strictly enforce and monitor the use of plastic bags and Styrofoam within the locality and firmly impose fines and penalties against violators; (iii) acquire an appropriate weighing scale for the Central MRF or contract an independent firm offering weighing services to determine the accurate weight of wastes collected by the hauler, and instruct the Central MRF personnel to maintain a logbook to record the wastes collected by the hauler; (iv) provide personal protective equipment to waste collectors and monitoring teams to ensure their health protection; (v) resume the provision of separate garbage trucks for biodegradable and non-biodegradable wastes and/or use of separate schedules for specific types of wastes, to ensure awareness of residents and establishments; or of separate schedules or routes for different types of waste; (vi) operate the Central MRF in compliance with environmental standards and best practices and ensure the effective and efficient utilization of equipment and machineries; and (vii) conduct waste characterization to improve the availability of information relevant to SWM planning decisions including data on demographics, waste compositions and volumes, and the condition of existing facilities and operations; and (b) require the CT and CA to establish a SWM Fund from the sale of organic and recyclable materials, which shall be accounted for separately from the General Fund to provide a stable funding source for recycling and composting activities, and other operating expenses of the Central MRF.
observations.reason: CENRO committed to review the provisions of the City’s Environmental Code which concerns the use of plastic bags and styrofoam within the City and to assess the need for any amendment as to imposable penalties. CENRO is still implemented conducting trial periods and logistics to successfully implement the separate schedules.

We recommended that Management enforce the collection of liquidated damages from suppliers and henceforth strictly observe the conditions of delivery extensions, especially on essential goods. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to: a. Direct the end-users to conduct project costing through market research/study of the goods to be procured and consider other cost components and gather information about the identified needs and corresponding budget to ensure that the ABC is set within the reasonable and fair cost of goods; b. The Bids and Awards Committee cease the practice of patronizing favored suppliers by faithfully and widely disseminate the RFQs to suppliers of known qualifications or distribute the invitations of bid to prospective bidders to intensify competition to avail of the most competitive prices of the goods; and c. Require the establishment of an IAS tasked with evaluating current procurement policies and procedures to enhance transparency, competition, and equity throughout the procurement process. Implement price benchmarking initiatives to compare the PE's expenditures with prevailing market rates for comparable goods. Promptly notify end-users and the BAC of any identified instances of overpricing.

observations.managementAction: Report on Fund Utilization and Status of Program/Project Implementation for various Local Government Support Funds (LGSF) was posted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management augment the Accounting Unit's personnel to specifically handle the preparation of the bank reconciliation statements and record the book reconciling items in the books. observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor as chairman of the Local School Board including the members of the Board to ascertain available School Board funds, review the School Improvement and Education Development Plan, formulate viable projects, programs, and activities, and maximize the use of available SEF for the benefits of the schools and the students.
The Municipal Accountant transfers the unexpended/unobligated balance of QRF and DRRMF-MOOE to the Trust Fund Books at year-end to present fairly the affected accounts in the financial statements;

We recommended that Management, thru the offices involved in the distribution of goods, submit the original copies of the distribution lists of beneficiaries or recipients as a basis for confirmation. We recommended and the Management agreed that the hiring of individual consultants undergoes the proper procurement procedures following the guidelines under Sections 1.0 and 2.0 of DBM Circular Letter No. 2017-09, dated May 16, 2017.
observations.managementAction: Consultant hiring was halted in the calendar year 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management agree to secure first a written concurrence of the COA before the hiring of a private lawyer in accordance with the existing rules and regulations; otherwise, the payment of consultancy service will be disallowed in the audit.
observations.managementAction: The hiring of private lawyer was halted in the CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: The Municipal Mayor consider appointing a regular General Services Officer to manage the supply and property functions closely; and The designated GSO to inspect the goods delivered and issue the PAR or ICS for equipment or tangible items in accordance with the existing rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: The GSO was designated by Management and property management functions closely; and assigned responsibilities to him.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer immediately stop the practice of allowing the JOs to collect taxes and fees in compliance with the existing regulations.

We recommended that the Management require the Inventory Committee to undertake the required physical inventory of PPE accounts according to the guidelines and procedures stated in COA Circular No. 2020-006 and submit to the Auditor an updated inventory report not later than January 31 of each year.
observations.managementAction: Reiterated in this year’s report

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to submit the required documents and strictly comply with the provisions of the GPPB Resolution No. 018-2006 whenever it implements infrastructure projects by the administration through Pakyaw System.
observations.managementAction: Post audit of CY 2021 DVs disclosed that the same practice is still being followed by the agency.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive augment the personnel of the Accounting Office to closely focus on the preparation of the updated Bank Reconciliation Statements. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant ensures that the preparation of the BRS is given priority in consonance with the existing accounting regulation.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to update her cashbook regularly. We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer reconcile the Cash-Local Treasury account’s subsidiary ledger and cashbook quarterly as required by existing regulations.

We reiterate our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor requires the Inventory Committee to undertake the required physical inventory of PPE accounts according to the guidelines and procedures stated in COA Circular No. 2020-006 and submit to the Auditor an updated inventory report not later than January 31 of each year.

We recommended that the Municipal LDRRM Officer consider in the formulation of disaster risk reduction management programs/projects/activities the savings from previous years’ completed LDRRMF Capital Outlay and to maximize the utilization of the fund.
We also recommended that the Municipality through its LDRRM Council to review its LDRRM Plan, consolidate all unexpended funds from previous years and formulate viable projects and activities to be implemented in the ensuing year.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to evaluate the viability of the previous year’s projects otherwise, direct the latter and the Municipal Engineer to expedite the implementation of the previous year’s 20% DF projects to ensure optimum utilization of the fund.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor directs the Municipal Engineer and other Offices that administer the 20% DF’s programs, projects, and activities to furnish the Municipal Planning and Development Officer with the status report of implemented PPAs for him to monitor the utilization of the fund.

We recommended that the Management, through the Municipal Engineer, require the Contractor to rectify the noted deficiencies; otherwise, the equivalent cost of defects/failures will be disallowed in the audit. Likewise, we recommend that henceforth, the Municipal Engineer inspect all projects meticulously and only recommend full payment if it is implemented according to the project's plans and specifications.
Furthermore, we recommended that the Management develop a preventive maintenance program to address the water supply interruption caused by leakages and equipment failure for better services to the project recipients.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to recognize the financial assistance of this kind as trust liability in the GF in consonance with regulation. Likewise, we recommend that the Municipal Budget Officer properly appropriate the fund through budget legislation following the DBM circular.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to only recognize AP in the books when the goods are accepted, services are rendered, and the corresponding bills are received from the contractor or supplier.
We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer records the obligation in the RAAO only when the agency actually incurs commitment.

We recommended and the BAC agreed to specify in the Resolution the mode of an alternative method to be adopted.  We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that the Resolution provides a specific alternative method to be used in accordance with the existing guidelines.

We recommended and the Management agreed that:
The MSWDO furnishes the Municipal Accounting Office with a detailed list of donations in-kind received by the municipality.
The Municipal Accounting Office recognizes in the books the donation in-kind in accordance with existing regulations.

We recommended and the Municipal Health Officer agreed to furnish the Municipal Accountant with a detailed inventory list of vaccines received by the Municipality for proper recognition in the books, in accordance with the accounting standards.

We recommended and the MSWD Officer agreed to review the list of beneficiaries and consider preparing a supplemental payroll to replace those who cannot be located anymore and those listed twice in the original payroll.
We also recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to liquidate immediately outstanding cash advances and settle or refund the remaining cash in her possession.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to maintain a separate cashbook for cash advances for each fund.  We also recommend that the cashbook be updated and regularly reconciled with accounting records.

We recommended and the Municipal Health Officer agreed to observe and expedite the use of about-to-expire vaccines to prevent wastage and formulate internal control policies necessary to minimize the occurrence of expired/damaged vaccines.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer immediately stop the practice of allowing the JOs to collect taxes and fees in compliance with the existing regulations.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor assign additional regular employees to the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to augment personnel complement to be assigned the collection function.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to provide the Acting Municipal Treasurer with a safe vault or secured storeroom to keep and safeguard the accountable forms in accordance with the existing regulations in the safekeeping of assets.

and Department of the  Interior  and  Local  Government

to the COVID-19 emergency.

We recommended that management update the Revenue  Code  of  the  City
Government of Cauayan pursuant to Section 191  of  R.A.  No.  7160  to

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to enroll and avail the “WeAccess System” of Land Bank of the Philippines to access the bank statements online so that BRS can be timely prepared and submitted to the Auditor for verification and accurate reporting of the Cash in Bank balance in the FS.

We recommended that the Management require the Inventory Committee to undertake the required physical inventory of PPE accounts according to the guidelines and procedures stated in COA Circular No. 2020-006 and submit to the Auditor an updated inventory report not later than January 31 of each year.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agree to make a detailed review of the accounts and determine the cause/s of the unreconciled amounts.  Thereafter, make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books of accounts to present the correct balances of the affected accounts.  Henceforth, exercise prudence in recording and classifying the Municipality’s financial transactions to ensure that the balances of the reciprocal accounts are at all times equal.

We recommended that the Management thru the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office submits the original copies of the distribution lists of goods to the beneficiaries or recipients as a basis for confirmation.  Also, require the Accounting Office to ensure that the itemized list of goods distributed are attached to the DVs for ease of verification.

We recommended that the BAC update or revise their APP and post it on the GPPB online portal.  Also, require the BAC to post the NOA or POs to the PE website and in the GPPB online portal for Emergency Procurement under the Bayanihan Act as required by the GPPB Circular.
We further recommended that the BAC or the Accounting Office furnish the Audit Team the copies of accreditation documents of the local suppliers and ensure that accredited suppliers strictly comply with the requirements of the GPPB Guidelines for Emergency Procurement.

We recommended that the Management thru the Municipal GSO issues the ICS or PAR over these properties.
Likewise, we recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to require ICS or PAR as one of the supporting documents for the payment of procured properties and equipment used for the management of COVID-19 disease so that accountability of the end-users is established.

We recommended that the Management thru the Special Disbursing Officers submit the lists of recipients/patients as a basis for confirmation.  Also, require the Accounting Office that the provision of food and other supplies in the operation of quarantine facility and hospital are provided with a list of recipients to assess the appropriateness of expenses.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor allocate the unutilized LDRRM Fund after five years to the social services program of the Municipality in consonance with COA Circular No. 2012-02.

We recommended and the Management agreed to exert effort on titling the parcel of land in the name of the municipal government.  Henceforth, see to it that the land title is first transfer in the name of the LGU before full payment is made.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to submit the required documents and strictly comply with the provisions of the GPPB Resolution No. 018-2006 whenever it implements infrastructure projects by administration through Pakyaw System.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Planning and Development Office ensure that the Municipality's AIP includes the detailed breakdown by clearly specifying each project to be prioritized for implementation and facilitate its inclusion in the Annual Budget, and directs the Offices concerned to expedite the implementation of all projects funded by 20% DF to ensure optimum utilization of the fund.
We also recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Officer closely monitor and evaluate the implementation of the different development projects in accordance with the AIP for timely delivery of basic and value-added services and benefits to its constituents.

We recommended that Management through the Project Focal Person closely monitor and hasten the implementation of the project.  Likewise, require the MPDO to adequately plan the project to identify ahead of the possible causes of delay and to provide an alternative course of action.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to records the procurement of drugs and medicines as inventory and takes up the corresponding expenses when the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) are submitted by the requisitioning office.  Likewise, an adjusting entry should be taken up to close the expense account and to adjust the liability account at year-end if the inventory is charged against the Trust Fund.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management inform the Audit Team of any retirement or movement of Accountable Officers so that this Office can conduct the mandatory cash examination as required under existing regulations.
We likewise recommended that the Management thru the Municipal Treasurer to require the subject AOs to settle their unaccounted accountable forms, otherwise face possible charges as they are not yet cleared of their accountabilities as AOs.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor and the Acting Municipal Treasurer immediately stop the practice of signing checks ahead of a duly processed and approved disbursement voucher/transaction.
Designate proper officials to act as counter-signatories of checks in case of their absence so as not to hamper the operation of the LGU.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require each Collecting Officer to prepare and submit the Monthly Report of Accountability for accountable forms.
We also recommended that the Acting Municipal Treasurer prepare and submit the consolidated Monthly Report of Accountability for accountable forms in compliance with the existing regulations.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to maintain the Cash in Bank-Cashbook for all funds in compliance with existing regulations, and for proper monitoring of the Cash in Bank balance.

We recommended and the Management agreed to provide the Acting Municipal Treasurer with safe or vault to safeguard cash collections, accountable forms as well as other important documents solely for her use.

We recommended that Management thru the Acting Municipal Treasurer to refrain from issuing accountable forms to personnel who are not duly bonded with the Bureau of Treasury, and transfer of accountable forms from one person to another, especially not duly bonded should be strictly prohibited.
Likewise, the Acting Municipal Treasurer should ensure that only regular and bonded employees should be authorized to collect revenues.
Furthermore, proper control procedure should be put in place over the accountable forms that is, the logbook should be duly signed by the requisitioning-collector acknowledging receipt of a particular series of accountable forms.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor assign a different person under the Accounting Office to maintain the subsidiary records of water consumers/customers.
Likewise, the Municipal Accountant should take-up the receivables from consumers in the books of accounts as these are already considered revenue although not yet collected by the agency.

We recommended that Management require the project supervisor to require each laborer to submit the DTR and Accomplishment Reports to support the services being paid.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant process only claims that have complete supporting documents, otherwise be held personally responsible and accountable thereof.

We recommended that the Management require the LDRRM Council to review their LDRRM Plan, consolidate all unexpended funds from previous years, and formulate viable projects and activities to be implemented in the ensuing year.
We also recommended that the LDDRM Officer ensures the timely utilization of the fund in order to improve the LGU's ability to prepare for and respond to threats of calamities and disasters.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Disaster and Risk Reduction Management Council to consider in the formulation of disaster risk reduction management programs/projects/activities the previous years’ unexpended balance of LDRRMF.  Likewise, ensure that these programs/projects/activities are implemented in the ensuing years to attain maximum utilization of the fund and enhance the agency’s disaster preparedness and response capabilities.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the Municipal LDRRM Officer and/or the Municipal Accountant to regularly prepare a monthly report on the utilization of the LDRRMF and submit the same to the Audit Team within the prescribed period for assessment and monitoring of the implementation of the projects/programs/projects, so that any setbacks may be brought to the attention of concerned officials the soonest possible time for their immediate and appropriate action.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to draw JEV to correct the affected accounts as follows:
Dr.			Cr.
Due from NGO/PO	   P775,000.00
Prior Period Adjustment (instead of expense)        P775,000.00
We also recommended that the Accounting Office maintain a subsidiary ledger to record the PO's account, to monitor the payment, and to support the general ledger control account, and require appropriate liquidation of the funds granted to POs.

We recommended that Management require the BAC to disseminate widely the Notice of Bid among qualified suppliers/contractors and consider postponement of bidding if only a single bidder has submitted their bid proposal to promote a competitive bidding process.
Likewise, we recommended that the BAC periodically review the history of bid tenders for particular procurement/project and discern suspicious patterns of bid collusion among suppliers/contractors and impose sanctions thereto if warranted.

We recommended that Management require the Inventory Committee to prepare and submit to the Auditor an updated inventory report on the physical count of the properties not later than January 31 of each year pursuant to Section 124 of the NGAS Manual, Volume I.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to ensure that the Municipality's AIP includes the detailed breakdown by clearly specifying each project to be prioritized for implementation, and facilitate its inclusion in the Annual Budget.  Furthermore, directs the Offices concerned to expedite the implementation of all projects funded by 20% DF to ensure optimum utilization of the fund.
We also recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Officer closely monitor and evaluate the implementation of the different development projects in accordance with the AIP for the timely delivery of basic and value-added services and benefits to its constituents.

We recommended that Management appoint a BUB Focal Person to closely supervise and monitor the implementation of all BUB projects by conducting a timely evaluation to avoid delays or to complete the projects on time.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to make a detailed review of the accounts and determine the cause/s of the unreconciled amounts.  Thereafter, make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books of accounts to present the correct balances of the affected accounts.  Henceforth, exercise prudence in recording and classifying the Municipality’s financial transactions to ensure that the balances of the reciprocal accounts are at all times equal.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to draw JEV to correct the affected accounts in the Trust Fund books, as follows:
Dr.			Cr.
Construction in Progress	   7,477,835.75
Due to NGA				P5,480,255.33
Due to LGU				   1,997,580.42
We also recommended that henceforth, the prescribed journal entries should be observed to record the fund transferred from NGAs and its related transactions in the books for a fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements.

In view, therefore, it is recommended that the Municipality, specifically the Local School Board to formulate, intensify and expedite the implementation of projects, programs, and activities relative to the operation of public schools in the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipality, through its GAD Focal Person to intensify and expedite the implementation of GAD programs and activities.  Likewise, the GAD Plan and Accomplishment Report must contain quantifiable performance results comparable with the performance indicator or target to gauge the agency’s accomplishment during the year.
Thus, we recommended that the LGU prepares the LSWMP and ensure that the identified projects, programs and activities be implemented on time.

      account that has reported collections without projection in their next
      annual budget to properly estimate income of the City Government;  and
      (2) maintain its commendable efforts in order to sustain its increased
      revenue collection.

      Fire Code Revenues from the system and let the BFP take charge of  the
      assessment and  collection  of  the  prescribed  fire  code  taxes  in
      accordance with Section 13 of Republic Act (R.A.) No.  9514  otherwise
      known as Fire Code of the Philippines of 2008.

      whether or not the transfer of funds received  from  the  BFP  already
      include those amounts corresponding to the 20% collections of the City
      Government in behalf of the national government from CYs 2010 to  2019
      or pertains only to the separate collection of the BFP.  In such case,

      possibility to establish a claim for  the  City  Government  from  the
      National Government.

4. We recommended that management reclassify the recorded expense account
      Consultancy Services to Donations in accordance with Annex  B  of  COA
      Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

8. We reiterated our recommendation that management:


      • Require  the  City  General  Services  Officer  to  prioritize  the
        registration and transfer of title of the land to ensure  ownership
        and to fully protect the interest of the City  in  compliance  with

      • Require the City Accountant to update her record thru inclusion  of
        the value of lots owned by the LGU in  the  books  of  accounts  to
        arrive at an  accurate  balances  of  Land  and  Government  Equity
        accounts.

4.11  The team further recommended that the information on  the  fair  value
      provided for the unrecorded land properties be properly  disclosed  in
      the notes to the financial statements.

3. We recommended that management:


     a) request from the source agency for authority to use the excess fund
        for purposes as it may allow; or in the alternative


     b)  remit the unexpended balance to the source agency.

5. We recommended that management monitor and ensure  the  implementation
      and completion of all the  LGU’s  programs,  projects  and  activities
      funded under  the  20%  Development  Fund  to  immediately  serve  the
      intended purposes or uses thereof to the constituents as  mandated  in
      DILG-DBM Joint Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

13. The team further reiterated the general  rule  that  no  cash  advance
      shall be given unless for a legally authorized specific  purpose.  The
      cash advance shall be reported  on  and  liquidated  as  soon  as  the
      purpose for which it was given has been served.

5. We recommended that management:

      1. submit legal basis for allowing the grant of advances on salaries,
         wages,  allowances,  terminal  leave  benefits  and  communication
         allowance;


      2. stop the practice of advancing payments  of  salaries,  wages  and
         allowances, terminal leave benefits  and  communication  allowance
         should there be no legal basis for the grant thereof; and



      3. collect  the  remaining  balance  of  the  unsettled  advances  of
         ₱267,358.73 as at year-end.

5. We reiterated our recommendation that management strictly adhere  with
      the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 76-34 and 2009-001.  Submission of
      perfected contracts (Purchase Orders and Contracts) to auditor must be
      strictly complied with.

4. We reiterated our recommendation that management inform the COA office
      for the deliveries of procured  equipment,  goods  and  materials  for
      proper inspection of the items within 24 hours as provided  under  COA
      Circular No. 2009-002.

3. We reiterated our recommendation that management strictly adhere  with
      the provisions of COA Circular No. 75-6 and ensure that  all  vehicles
      and equipment owned by the City be marked with “For Official Use Only”
      followed by the name of the City Government of  Cauayan  to  safeguard
      government property against potential mismanagement and misuse.

5. We recommended that management require the Bids and  Awards  Committee
      (BAC) to prepare and submit the Procurement Monitoring Report  to  the
      Head of the Procuring Entity (HoPE) for his approval  and  submit  the
      same to the GPPB in compliance with the provisions stated  in  Section
      12.2 of the Revised Implementing Rules and  Regulations  of  R.A.  No.
      9184.

6. We recommended that management  conduct  a  study  and  explore  other
      alternatives and measures that maybe adopted by the City Government to
      ensure the positive result of operations of the market enterprise.

      Reduction in the cost of personnel services  relative  to  the  market
      operations may be considered to at  least  break  even  level,  should
      positive result is not possible within the  first  ten  years  of  the
      lease agreement.

3. We  reiterated  our  recommendation  that  proper  monitoring  of  the
      maintenance of the books be instituted by the focal person of the City
      and require the PG  to  rent  out  the  machineries  pursuant  to  the
      approved operational plan.

5. We recommended that management refrain from accommodating expenditures
      which do not fall within  the  recommended  programs,  activities  and
      projects chargeable against SEF and strictly adhere to  the  pertinent
      provisions of Section 272 of R.A. No. 7160 and Item 4.0  of  DepEd-DBM
      and DILG Joint Circular No. 01 series of 2017 in  the  utilization  of
      SEF.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Inventory Committee initiate the conduct of physical count of its properties and submit a report thereon pursuant to the existing regulations to establish a fair valuation of PPE accounts in the financial statements.  

We also recommended that the Inventory Committee utilize the subsidiary ledgers of the e-NGAs at the Accounting Office in the inventory taking and later reconcile their physical inventory report against that of the subsidiary records.

Furthermore, the physical inventory report should contain the actual condition of the assets whether it is unserviceable, scrap or already junk so that proper adjustments in the books can be made.
